sentences.text: Yes.
That's the only experience.
There were some talk about it, that there was a case that some Jews were killed by the partisans themselves.
But it wasn't spread too much.
Occasionally, you could hear.
I didn't see it personally.
But we talked with other people that told us who were much longer in the partisans than I was.
And also, there was once a case, was also like a family camp, maybe four, five miles away-- four or five kilometers away from ours.
And the local police attacked them at-- in the morning.
They had no guns.
And they killed every one of them in the forest, the whole group.
We were later on visiting.
We saw some graves over there in the woods.
And the, the military actually are partisans, which they find out about it so burned the whole village with a lot of people, too.
Cause they know from [INAUDIBLE] they were coming.
That was the only incident which I know about it.
No, I was just told from all the partisans.
No, there were some older men, which had girlfriends in, in the camp.
But there were a few young boys, which we did not bother yet.
But otherwise, I mean, even it was miserable, physically, it was not comfortable.
But psychologically, we again [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] free human beings.
We were not afraid that we might get killed or sent on an expedition. exposition or-- or any jobs they gave us, we were gladly taking it.
In fact, everybody was volunteering.
But, it's almost like a new human being that you're free.
It's hard to understand when a person is imprisoned, knowing that if you do anything what they tell you not to do, you'll get killed.
Any move, you get killed.
And we didn't have that feeling.
All we wanted is fight the Nazis and, and, and get home.
Yes.
Yes, yes, Red Army, yeah.
Yes.
Before we joined, there was still a battle going on.
And all the partisans-- there was a big cemetery.
We were behind the stones, you know, at, at the battlefield.
After that, it took us maybe an hour or two.
And the shooting stopped.
It looks like the regular Russian army took over the whole area over there.
And the Germans were giving up.
They were prisoners of war.
That day, we came in.
We didn't know what to do.
We had all kind of guns.
And they told us, keep it on the side.
And first of all, they took our names.
And they gave us irregular uniforms.
It didn't fit.
But we had our regular Russian uniform, with the, with the red star.
And we were absorbed in the Russian army.
The following couple days, we were still in the city.
Some had enough training.
Some didn't have enough training.
It didn't bother the Russian army.
It didn't bother them.
Because they assumed that we all qualified fighters from a long time ago.
And naturally, when you're in the army, it's a different, different way of living.
Even with-- also, a lot of them were really rough, very primitive.
At daytime was fighting going on.
At night, we had the regular military kitchen.
Everybody got this rationing.
We start eating at night.
Many times, the artillery used to still shoot at us.
But nobody paid any attention.
And we were going forward, going west.
We already, we already passed Byelorussia, going towards Warsaw, the, the whole army.
I was in the 348th [NON-ENGLISH].
It means this is the name of the division, 348 [NON-ENGLISH] division.
And naturally, that was a different battle already, because we went forward.
We had enough guns.
We had enough artillery, tanks.

But once we got to a place maybe 100, 100 kilometers west of-- between Bialystok and Baranowicze going west, we were all-- our division was on the north front going towards the Baltic Sea, the whole division.
And we were way down.
And on top was a hill.
And the Germans were over there.
And every morning before just start getting a little light, the-- we opened up the Katyushas.
It's a, it's a horrible-- it's a horrible noise.
It's unbelievable for a human being.
But what means a Katyusha?
Katyusha is automatic artillery.
They used to soften up the German infantry.
So before the-- our infantry, the Russian infantry, had to go forward, they were softening them, so-called, with, with the artillery.
When we got close to the hill, there's lots, there's lots of German dead people.
And also, a lot of them were still in the trenches.
And lots of our, our soldiers were killed, too.
There was once a-- this was the first real battle which I participate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we took the hill.
Then we went again, maybe 15, 20, 30 miles forward without even [INAUDIBLE], no fighting at all.
It looks like the Germans left at night.
They used to escape at night.
And the infantry and the tanks would still go forward.
And a few days later, we had-- it looks like the Germans stopped again.
And we had to do the same thing.
And one of my closest friends, he was a Russian.
He was 18 or 19 years old.
And we were in the trenches.
And I told him, look-- he wanted to see what's going on, at least up the hill?
I told him, no, just stay that way.
Don't.
And somehow, he had the hunch he wanted to see.
He picked up his head.
And he got a bullet straight in the head.
That's a horrible experience.
And we would go forward all the way.
Almost our division came close to my hometown of Mlawa.
The troops, we went through already Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we're going west.
In a, in a big battle, I got hit.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I got injured in the right hand.
I almost lost it.
But in the battlefield, I had later on a machine gun.
I was injured, but the, the help-- even in the war, the medical help was not so hot.
It was not so good.
I, I could not leave my machine gun, run back to the field hospital.
I had to carry my machine gun on my backs.
The blood was running.
And when I got to the field hospital, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took off the machine gun off me.
Because I couldn't take it off myself.
And I got the first help.
They saw it's a very bad injury.
So they put us aside.
Because there was-- were two kind of injured people.
If you were lightly injured, they kept you in the field hospital.
If you were very badly injured, leg, a head, or whatever, they used to take us to the train station and take it back way deep to Russia.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in end of '44, I was on a transport with my right hand.
And I went two days and two nights in the train, military train.
All were injured, were a few thousand people.
And we wind up, and its name is Michurinsk.
It's after Moscow.
It's east of Moscow.
No, Michurinsk.
It was a big hospital.
There were thousands of injured soldiers over there.
And I was operated over there.
And I got a cast.
And I was there almost six months.
No.
There's no such thing as morphine.
It was-- I don't know what they did.
But it was really painful.
I didn't have the hand in a cast yet.
It was bandaged heavy.
I got some medications.
But after, I think, two nights and one day, or two days and two nights, we were travelling east.
It used to be at one time, used to be a palace before the revolution.
And they converted to a, a regular military hospital.
There were thousands and thousands of soldiers from all over.
And over there, it was six months.
And--
No, no, no.
It was, was broken, the, the bone.
And if this was be from older person, doctor told me you'd have to cut it off.
But luckily, she told me the nerves [? would count ?] part of the bones.
Because I got hit with a [NON-ENGLISH].
It's, it's a bullet which each piece explodes again.
Russian army.
No.
No, we did not.
No.
We didn't know about it.
No, we did not-- we never had the time to talk to the people.
Once you're in the service in the army, you don't go, go start finding out what's happening.
You go forward.
You got an order to go.
And all-- every human being, every-- as long as you can walk, you have to obey.
He name was Ivan.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It-- he had-- he was not actually a Russian.
He was from Kavkaz.
There was a lot in our division, were a lot of people from the Muslim countries.
Were the Republic of the Soviet Union, but there were Muslims.
Kazakhstan, gosh, there's so many of them.
Tashkent.
Tashkent is a city in Kazakhstan.
But in that area were a lot of them, young boys.
Yes.
In fact, we-- in fact, I do remember that before every battlefield, we used to get vodka and, and pork or, or, or-- he never ate any pork in the service.
And vodka, yes, he took a little bit, but nothing else.
We used to exchange.
I used to exchange with him.
Yes.
I don't.
Gosh.
It will come to me.
But at this moment-- I know Ivan, we used to call him.
But it doesn't mean anything.
I mean, but--
Yes.
I, I will.
Maybe it will come to me.
Of course, this is the first time in 52 years, I'm opening my mind.
I could never cry [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in so many years, until now, telling the story to the human race.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What I went through during the war, it's, it's hard to believe that a human being can be-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] can be so strong and tell the experience and witness the crimes the Nazis committed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm OK.
[PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Now, after I, after I got-- after I was released from hospital--
I beg your pardon?
Six months.
My hand was stiff.
I still had some pieces of metal in my hand, which, almost a year or more after the, after the war, it was still coming out.
They were pulling it out.
The doctor used to pull out.
And I went home to find out what's happening to my family.
I know my father is dead.
I hoped my mother and my little sisters will be alive.
I didn't worry so much about the uncles and grandfather, because I know they were grown up people.
But my little sisters and my mother-- [CRYING] Unfortunately, I came into the city.
And I was one, I was one of the first Jews who came back after the war to Mlawa.
I, I looked around.
There were no Jews.
The, the Poles were surprised to see that I am in a Russian uniform, even though I have no insignias.
Just, I didn't have anything else to wear.
I have no civilian clothes.
They didn't give us any civilian clothes.
Whatever you had in the service, you could go home and, and, it and worked.
In meantime, there were already Russian headquarters.
I went over there.
I registered myself.
And then, I registered myself in the Polish army, in the, in the, like, draftboard.
And I told them, I need some help.
I'm, I'm a injured person.
And I wanted to find out.
And then I got horrible stories.
People were still coming back from Auschwitz.
I had people coming back.
And I start finding out what happens from '39 til the war ended for me.
The war was still going on.
It was going west.
But in the spring-- I mean, pardon me, in the winter, early winter of '45, Poland was liberated.
The Russian army were already in Germany.
And we start finding out the tragedy of what happens.
I got some reports that in 1942, there was a ghetto Mlawa.
They separated the young women with the children first at Treblinka.
The men went to Auschwitz.
I have pictures from some of the people.
And I find out the horrible thing which happens to, to my family and to all the, all the Jewish people in my city.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And from that on, we had to start a new life.
I was alone.
I was a grown-up man, almost 20 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Polish army, since I was 20, was a draft age, they called me.
And I told them, look, I had my share already.
But when they saw, saw on the certificate that I'm injured person, I'm, I'm, I'm finished already.
And they let me go.
But they were already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poles illegally, they had guns already.
They were against the Russians.
At one night, they were looking for me.
They figured I must be a, a communist or something like that.
But luckily, I was not in that house.
And next day, I told them that they were looking for me.
I took off.
I never went back over there.
And I went with, with a Russian, Russian soldier.
I told him, I want to go.
So he gave me a lift.
And I went across the border from Poland to East Germany.
And then I came to-- I took the train.
I went to Berlin.
Then I got connected with other people that did the same thing what I did.
And that's the way we start off a new life.
It was empty.
There was one family living.
There was a double.
One family was living.
The other one was vacant.
Nobody was living in the house.
No.
I stayed-- there was a big house in the center of the city.
I think it was a hotel, or somehow.
And it was designated for the people coming back from concentration camp.
The local people know more than I did.
I didn't know anything about it.
There were camps.
There were gassing.
There were whatever it was.
I didn't-- I find out after the war.
No.
I never went inside.
No.
Because after so many years, I assumed that somebody else was living.
I didn't have no desire even to walk in the house.
Once I find out that nobody is alive, and there were a few thousand Jews-- a few thousand Jews in the city.
And then I got news that my mother was taken away in 1942, my mother and my little sisters.
So I figured, what I going to look for?
I wanted to get out as fast as I could.
I was in a DP camp, displaced person camp.
It was in the French sector, [GERMAN].
It wasn't a name.
It's used to be-- there's a factory across the street in Berlin-- you know, those wide city, wide boulevards.
And the people who worked in the factory, in the ammunition factory, used to live in those homes.
And all those, the whole project, was taken over by the UNRRA, [? for ?] the, for displaced persons.
I lived from '45 to '49.
No.
Oh, we used to make some money on the black market.
That's the only way you could make a living.
Well, we used to buy cigarettes from soldiers and sell it to the Germans.
That's the way we survived.
We used to get food in the camp over there.
But we didn't want to be in a camp again.
So it-- actually, it was more accommodations than a camp.
We could go anytime you wanted in or out.
It was not a camp.
They used to call it UNRRA camp.
But actually, it was-- it's apartment buildings.
We had, in every room, we had two or three people leaving.
It was livable.
I was undecided.
But I knew I have some distant relatives, or relatives, in, in, in, in New York.
So somehow, somebody from the American soldiers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told me, you can-- when I got home, I'll call them.
There is, there was a committee in New York City from my hometown, from Mlawa.
And they will try to help as many people as they can.
Because they knew more what's happening in the east than, than being over there.
And then I got a, a letter from somebody.
But I could not read English.
So I had to go translate that I had a cousin and an older uncle.
And he told me, we'll try to help you out to come to the United States.
And in '49, I left Berlin-- oh, before the blockade started off.
So I went to Frankfurt.
Frankfurt am Main, that's the big city in, in western Germany.
And I was living over there with a family, [?
Goldwasser. ?] And they were also in Auschwitz.
So I got even more information what's happening.
And, and after that, one day we got the orders that we qualified to go to the United States.
And that's the way I left through the, the war years.
He passed away already.
His daughter is still alive in Philadelphia.
I'm in touch with her.
No.
She was born in Germany after the war.
In, in New York.
In '49, June, '49.
Yes.
Well, it was strange.
See, the uncle was speaking Yiddish to me.
The cousins, they could-- they spoke a little bit, but not too much.
And they tried to, to help me out.
I have no trade.
I have no language.
But I didn't want to stay with them.
After a couple days, or I guess four or five days, I was in the, in the house over there.
They were living also in, in an apartment.
I just told them, I've got to try to do something on my own.
And I did.
Yes, I got married in '54.
Fanny Gradow.
Yes.
But she had different experience than I had.
I didn't know her before the war.
I met her in New York.
Yes, I have two children.
I have a son, Steven, and a daughter, Helene.
Yes.
My son is named after my, my in-laws, my, my wife's father.
My daughter is named after my name-- my father, because my mother had the same name as my, my wife.
So we couldn't use the same name in the Jewish religion.
That's why, Helene, my father's name was [INAUDIBLE] or Nochim.
[INAUDIBLE] we made Helene.
And I have two grandchildren.
Yeah.
My granddaughter is Sarah.
And my grandson is Elliot.
Yes.
Sarah [?
Dina, ?] my granddaughter, is named after my sister, the older sister.
And Elliot is named after my wife's uncle, which he passed away before Elliot was born.
Well, with the first job I got, I was in the garment industry.
Because I learned some trade in New York.
I used to go to school at night.
And they taught me how to be a cutter, how to cut fabrics, ladies', ladies' wear.
And when I came-- and I didn't like New York.
The weather was so, so rough on me.
I couldn't take it.
My nose used to bleed all the time in summertime.
I couldn't take the, the climate over there.
It's funny.
During the war, I was fine.
But I get to New York, I, I couldn't stand it.
And I came to Los Angeles.
I had a friend of mine also from Mlawa.
And I got the first job as a cutter.
They assumed that every man who, who comes from New York must be an excellent cutter.
And the first, the first week, I got about $25.
And I worked about 60 or 70 hours only.
And I learned the trade fast.
OK.
